title: Keep Up With AI
embedHtml: <iframe width="480" height="360" src="//www.youtube.com/embed/-YpRol8m5QQ" frameborder="0" allow="accelerometer; autoplay; clipboard-write; encrypted-media; gyroscope; picture-in-picture; web-share" referrerpolicy="strict-origin-when-cross-origin" allowfullscreen></iframe>
transcript: Hey there. I wanted to share an unpopular opinion. That is that you can keep up with AI and it's not even too hard. Okay, let me kind of give you some context behind this thought. So, obviously there is tons of new AI stuff coming out all the time and you can't keep up. That's kind of the trend of the talk. Now, I guess if I'm going to approach it from the idea of like, is there some tool out there that I don't know about that could help me do my job better than I do it? And the answer is of course there is yes. There's so many tools and I haven't tried them all and I etc etc. I don't have the time to try them all. If I tried all the tools, basically I would spend all my time trying tools. That's It's kind of like trying to watch all the YouTube videos because there's way more hours uploaded every day than there are hours in a day. So, um it's kind of the reality we live in. But the good thing is is that you can keep up with AI. And the reason I say this, if you have just a few main channels of AI news, then all the significant updates are going to hit the news. So basically, um, yeah, you see stuff like ChatGpt and I didn't catch on to chatbt instantly. Uh, I started seeing the word chat GBT for u probably a month before I clicked on it and was like, what is this chatgbt thing? What are all these articles thinking about? I mean, what what is this about? Because it just it meant nothing to me when I saw the headlines of it. But eventually, I was like, oh, this crazy new tool and this is going to change everything. Um, and I guess that's where I'm kind of going with this topic is just that if something is significant enough that you know all your competitors are going to take advantage of it and you're going to fall behind. Well, it's probably going to be big in the news. Everybody's going to be talking about it. So, same thing happened again with Open Claw. There's all this stuff about Open Claw and, you know, I looked at it, you know, I was a few days late or whatever. I mean, oh, it was Claudebot. I saw a few things about Claudebot and I was like, I don't know what that is. Eventually clicked on it. It's like, oh wow, this agent that can do things and then I looked I was like, okay, it's it's a security nightmare. So, I didn't do anything with that and there's all kinds of updates of course now coming from the main providers, Claude, Chat, GPT, and of course, Google's going to be trying to catch up as well um with agents that do things for you. Uh that's obviously where all this is going. And so as long as you're kind of watching, you see what tools are out there, then you know, you might be a month or two late, but um it's not going to be the end of the world. In that sense, you're not going to lose some major competitive edge because you found out about a tool one month later. Like that's not how fast customers change who they're buying from just because another company happened to take on and utilize the new tool. Um that kind of thing just doesn't matter. So that's my talk for today. All the scare about, you know, you're going to fall behind. Um there's definitely truth to it. There's definitely truth to it if you ignore it. Um, but if you are somewhat interested and you kind of keep up with the tools and you try new things, um, I guess that's the most important thing of the current age is to check the big updates and look into what they're about and see if it's something just have some general idea of what's going on and if something big happens, you'll eventually catch on to it. Um, if you are staying the news sources. Cool. That's it for today.

title: Spring Cleaning
embedHtml: <iframe width="480" height="360" src="//www.youtube.com/embed/HoGdiOisQJE" frameborder="0" allow="accelerometer; autoplay; clipboard-write; encrypted-media; gyroscope; picture-in-picture; web-share" referrerpolicy="strict-origin-when-cross-origin" allowfullscreen></iframe>
transcript: How's it going? I was doing some spring cleaning recently and I wanted to share some little tips of things I figured out. So, first of all, obviously, um, you want to clean out everything's garbage. Um, so one of the things I figured out about this is if you move everything, so let's say you're cleaning out a drawer and you move everything in that drawer to another place, um, it becomes splashingly obvious uh, really quickly. You'll see things that are just obviously garbage and you kind of forgot they existed. So I guess the tip there is just to move everything um, out of the space that you are organizing. Uh secondly is going to be digitize. That's a commonly known one, but it should be noted and there's a lot of good apps that you can just scan a picture in and make a PDF out of something and upload it to the cloud and so you have all these papers and you can just digitize them. The third one is the most interesting that is consumables that have fallen out of the stream of consumption. And so this is going to be like all those things in your freezer that you forgot about. So it's something you need to eat, but you just don't have like a date when you're actually going to eat them. And so they end up getting thrown away sometimes. up. Um, another good example is any kind of fancy gift consumable item that you just need to use and maybe you're saving it for a special occasion. So, I just want to say this, today is a special day. So, that's your inspiration to start using up those things that you're saving for some special occasion that doesn't specifically exist. Of course, if you are saving it for an occasion that is actually on the calendar, then that's fine. Um, but if it's just kind of tucked away to some unknown, let's do this someday, we forgot about it, then you should just get those things out and use them. Whether it's like a candle or whatever, you know, things like that. Hope this helps in your spring cleaning journey. Cheers.

title: Brutal Feedback
embedHtml: <iframe width="480" height="360" src="//www.youtube.com/embed/f4H4cPeB-UM" frameborder="0" allow="accelerometer; autoplay; clipboard-write; encrypted-media; gyroscope; picture-in-picture; web-share" referrerpolicy="strict-origin-when-cross-origin" allowfullscreen></iframe>
transcript: A random concept I've just been thinking about is what if everyone could take brutal feedback and just like, hey, that's fine. Um, you know, just bash me over the head. Um, it's just an interesting thought. I I started thinking about this when um I watched something where it was like, oh yeah, kids will give brutal feedback. And it's very true. Like kids don't have kind of the social knowledge of like, hey, you're going to say something that's going to hurt somebody. Um but at some point along the way uh children start to learn the impact of what feedback they're giving and they start to realize ah maybe I'll hurt this person so I don't want to hurt you know their feelings and so then all of a sudden we filter out all that feedback that we could give people and what that results in is that suddenly we don't get the insight into ourselves. that we probably really need. Um, of course, um, there's obviously a reason for how this works because obviously if we just felt bad things about ourselves all the time, uh, we wouldn't perform at a high level. We would just be, you know, get depressed or whatever because, you know, just, oh yeah, I'm a terrible person. But if we um if we could take feedback um I guess I think about the feedback loop a lot because the feedback loop is so critical um I mean especially I think about with automating things and you know you got to have a feedback loop say okay the automatedation didn't work here and so okay let's tweak it and then it gets better and better and better. Um, but it obviously applies with human just everything human as well. So like getting that feedback is so important about how do we how do we get the level of feedback that we need to become our best version of ourselves possible. Anyway, so yeah, so I started thinking, you know, what if we just could take feedback and, you know, just take it with no with no hard feelings. Um, it's an interesting thought because then we wouldn't have learned. We would just continue giving the feedback, of course. hearing all these fun

title: Video Automation Update
embedHtml: <iframe width="480" height="360" src="//www.youtube.com/embed/r_ecyy5VXj4" frameborder="0" allow="accelerometer; autoplay; clipboard-write; encrypted-media; gyroscope; picture-in-picture; web-share" referrerpolicy="strict-origin-when-cross-origin" allowfullscreen></iframe>
transcript: Okay, just a quick update on my automation that I've set up for the upload. So, I have done an additional step. So still I put it in a folder and then that automatically gets uploaded and then um the additional step I've done is it well it continues to get posted to my website and then I have an API called go out to get the transcript of the video and then post that on the website and now as you can see um there's a search bar where you can search the transcripts of every video that I post. So, I'm pretty excited about this update because now I've got all the text of the transcripts of all the videos posting to my website as well. So, just some fun automation stuff. Um, I love playing around with automations. See how little work we can do for how much of value. So, that's today's update.

title: Is plain chat the best?
embedHtml: <iframe width="480" height="360" src="//www.youtube.com/embed/oRjZ1WQjigs" frameborder="0" allow="accelerometer; autoplay; clipboard-write; encrypted-media; gyroscope; picture-in-picture; web-share" referrerpolicy="strict-origin-when-cross-origin" allowfullscreen></iframe>
transcript:
One thing I found interesting: I've tried a lot of things — custom GPTs, skills, various AI tools — and I found myself going back to just plain chat sometimes. Even just the auto, no-thinking model for a lot of questions. There's not really a right or wrong answer — some tasks definitely need thinking capabilities or more reasoning. But I guess what I take from this is that sometimes I've tried to automate something before it really needed to be. Like, let's make a custom tool just for this — but really, plain chat would work fine.

I guess it goes back to something I keep running into: do it manually first. That's the key rule I've come up with for automation. If you do something manually, you deeply understand how it works, and so you know what it's going to take to automate it.

I feel like I've jumped to automating everything before it really needed to be. A takeaway for myself: see the repetition maybe three or four times before looking to build a special tool — because I keep doing the exact same thing over and over, and at that point it makes sense to automate. But yeah, I'm probably just a bit too jumpy to automate everything. Anyway, it's a lot of fun.

title: AI Agent Capability Self-Awareness
embedHtml: <iframe width="480" height="360" src="//www.youtube.com/embed/SVwk9nLQECc" frameborder="0" allow="accelerometer; autoplay; clipboard-write; encrypted-media; gyroscope; picture-in-picture; web-share" referrerpolicy="strict-origin-when-cross-origin" allowfullscreen></iframe>
transcript:
In the age of AI, most ideas take me about one afternoon to accomplish. But the idea of taking a video, uploading it to a folder, and having it automatically show up on my website has taken me two afternoons. Big idea, I guess — apparently in modern calculations.

Anyway, we'll see if this shows up as expected.

I want to take a minute to talk about a video I saw just recently — it's been circulating around. A guy took ChatGPT Voice and asked it to start a stopwatch. Then there was a reaction from Sam Altman. He said he watched the video and said, "Oh, that feature is about a year out" — because ChatGPT Voice had said, "Okay, I'm starting a stopwatch." Then he said, "Okay, stop it." And ChatGPT said, "Okay, you took seven minutes" — but it had only been like 10 seconds. ChatGPT was just making it up. I was able to duplicate the same issue.

The interesting thing: Sam Altman was focused on the missing tool — the stopwatch. The ChatGPT Voice model simply does not have a stopwatch. That's different from how I think about the issue, because I'm in the middle of designing agents as well, and I run into this exact same problem. An agent says, "Oh, you want to do this? Sure, I can do that" — and then goes ahead and pretends like it will. In my case, it was gathering a bunch of information, and then you get to the end and it fails, because the agent didn't know what it was capable of doing.

The way I see it: go in there and tell your model it doesn't have a stopwatch capability yet, so it doesn't accept requests for tools it doesn't actually have.

Anyway, I just thought that was interesting. It's very relevant to what I do — we have to make these agents know what they're capable of so they don't annoy users. User experience is key in designing tools. That's my thoughts for today.

title: Hello World
embedHtml: <iframe width="480" height="360" src="//www.youtube.com/embed/TMyqjOFRVEc" frameborder="0" allow="accelerometer; autoplay; clipboard-write; encrypted-media; gyroscope; picture-in-picture; web-share" referrerpolicy="strict-origin-when-cross-origin" allowfullscreen></iframe>
transcript:
Hello world. What a technical pun.

The purpose of this video has nothing to do with being fancy or getting views — it has everything to do with proving that an automated workflow of uploading a video to YouTube will work.

We'll see if this shows up on my channel. If it does, then we know the first hello world experiment was successful. That's what this is all about.

My theory is that it should be very easy to upload new videos — I'm going to try to automate the whole process, so it's just a drag into a folder.

Alright, that's it for my hello world video.
